elements.sent-2411-001: Accurate motion control in the face of disturbances within complex environments remains a major challenge in robotics.
elements.sent-2411-002: Classical model-based approaches often struggle with nonlinearities and unstructured disturbances, while reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods can be fragile when encountering unseen scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose a novel framework, Neural Internal Model Control (NeuralIMC), which integrates model-based control with RL-based control to enhance robustness.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our framework streamlines the predictive model by applying Newton-Euler equations for rigid-body dynamics, eliminating the need to capture complex high-dimensional nonlinearities.
elements.sent-2411-005: This internal model combines model-free RL algorithms with predictive error feedback.
elements.sent-2411-006: Such a design enables a closed-loop control structure to enhance the robustness and generalizability of the control system.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on both quadrotors and quadrupedal robots, achieving superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, real-world deployment on a quadrotor with rope-suspended payloads highlights the framework’s robustness in sim-to-real transfer.
elements.sent-2411-010: Achieving stable and precise motion in complex, disturbed environments is essential for robots, such as quadrotors maintaining stable flight in wind fields and legged robots carrying loads over diverse terrains.
elements.sent-2411-011: This challenge, known as the robust control or adaptive control problem, has been extensively studied~\cite{acbook .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Classical adaptive control methods adjust control parameters in real-time to compensate for dynamic environmental changes, often using mathematical models of the system and disturbances~\cite{nguyen2018model,rivera1986internal,aastrom1973self,lin2000self , while robust control methods utilize more informative signals to enhance robustness, like Internal Model Control~\cite{francis1976internal .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite their guaranteed stability, these methods rely on accurately modeling system dynamics, which is challenging for nonlinear systems with unstructured disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-014: In contrast, reinforcement learning (RL) methods have shown remarkable success in continuous control across various robot morphologies, including skill learning for armed robots~\cite{2018-TOG-deepMimic,li2023learning , high-speed quadrotor racing~\cite{kaufmann2023champion,song2023reaching , and adaptive locomotion for legged robots~\cite{doi:10.1126/scirobotics.aau5872,kumar2021rma,long2023him,li2024reinforcement,radosavovic2024real .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Large-scale training with randomized dynamics and environmental parameters enables RL-based control methods to generalize across different conditions and environments~\cite{peng2017simtoreal,rudin2021learning , allowing robots to perform reliably even in previously unseen and highly varying scenarios.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{img/framework2.pdf} \caption{Predictive errors can be caused by mismatched system modeling and/or external disturbances.} \label{fig:pef}
elements.sent-2411-016: There has been a recent trend towards integrating model-based control with RL-based neural control to harness the strengths of both worlds~\cite{o2022neural,li2022bridging,long2023him,huang2023datt .}
elements.sent-2411-017: One representative framework is to use predicted future states or estimated disturbances as an additional input signal to obtain a more informative model-free RL policy.
elements.sent-2411-018: Long et al.~\cite{long2023him employ unsupervised contrastive representation learning to train a predictive encoder capable of estimating the next state in the latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Conversely, Huang et al.~\cite{huang2023datt use a handcrafted $\mathcal{L _1$ adaptive law~\cite{l1ac to explicitly estimate applied force disturbances, resulting in a robust trajectory tracking controller that can handle unexpected wind fields.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Although promising, integrating model-based and RL-based controls faces significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-021: Predictive models often struggle with variability when training data cannot cover real-world dynamics, and handcrafted disturbance estimators require precise tuning, limiting their feasibility for complex systems and adaptability to dynamic changes.
elements.sent-2411-022: For establishing a more general method, we need to develop a structure that effectively combines these controls to fully leverage their advantages and address these limitations.

elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we propose a general and flexible framework that integrates model-based and RL-based control.
elements.sent-2411-024: By simplifying the model to focus on the robot's main body and treating its dynamics as a rigid transformation, we bypass the high-dimensional nonlinearity of complex robot systems and environmental interactions.
elements.sent-2411-025: This simplification enables us to use an explicit predictive model without requiring parameter tuning, establishing a foundation for robust closed-loop control.
elements.sent-2411-026: We compute the predictive error—defined as the discrepancy between plant and model responses—as a feedback signal to the policy, enhancing robustness and generalizability through informative feedback and extensive training.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our framework, shown in \cref{fig:overview , can be easily integrated with other learning algorithms by incorporating this feedback loop.}
elements.sent-2411-028: To demonstrate the effectiveness and versatility of our method, we apply it to two prevalent robot morphologies: quadrotors, which demand precise 3D control, and quadrupedal robots, which require adaptive locomotion to navigate complex terrain interactions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Empirical results indicate that our framework can surpass recent state-of-the-art control methods in both domains.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, we conduct a real-world deployment on a quadrotor with various rope-suspended payloads to illustrate its robustness in sim-to-real transfer.
elements.sent-2411-031: Classical adaptive controllers require estimating system parameters in a closed-loop manner to adjust control actions in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-032: Neural-Fly~\cite{o2022neural incorporated pre-trained representations to enhance rapid online adaptation through deep learning, achieving stable flight in previously unseen wind conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Internal Model Control (IMC)~\cite{francis1976internal,rivera1986internal is a general structure for robust control that uses a predefined model to predict system responses and estimate disturbances.}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, applying IMC to more complex and nonlinear systems is challenging due to the difficulty of crafting reliable models for such systems.
elements.sent-2411-035: Long et al.~\cite{long2023him further proposed Hybrid Internal Model, in which they leverage a learning-based internal model with unsupervised contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The learned model serves as a predictive encoder for estimating the system responses in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-037: Despite their advantages, these methods either rely on explicit disturbance estimation or are heavily influenced by prediction accuracy, which can significantly affect performance if compromised.
elements.sent-2411-038: With the rapid advancement of highly parallel simulators~\cite{rudin2021learning,mittal2023orbit,xu2023omnidrones , reinforcement learning (RL) methods are increasingly demonstrating powerful capabilities to achieve robust control, including quadrotors~\cite{zhang2023learning, huang2023datt and legged robots~\cite{doi:10.1126/scirobotics.aau5872, kumar2021rma, long2023him, li2024reinforcement, radosavovic2024real .}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Unlike classical model-based control, these learning-based methods often use model-free RL algorithms like PPO~\cite{schulman2017proximal to train neural controllers in large-scale randomized environments.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Koryakovskii et al.~\cite{modelplant2018ral proposed to incorporate an RL-baesd policy to generate residual actions to compensate nonlinear model predictive control in model-mismatch cases.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Rapid Motor Adaptation (RMA)~\cite{kumar2021rma is a pure RL-based methods, which predicts environmental parameters in the latent space using state-action histories through a teacher-student training procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This approach has been extended to quadrotors~\cite{zhang2023learning , bipedal robots~\cite{kumar2022adapting , and humanoid robots~\cite{radosavovic2024real .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: A follow-up study found that a dual-history architecture, leveraging both short-term and long-term input/output history, achieves superior performance with a straightforward one-stage training process~\cite{li2024reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Conversely, DATT~\cite{huang2023datt incorporates an \(\mathcal{L _1\) adaptation law~\cite{l1ac to estimate forces applied to quadrotors directly, which are then used as policy inputs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Despite its effectiveness with precise parameter tuning, this method is sensitive to handcrafted settings and challenging to generalize to complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we present a general framework that bridges model-based and RL-based control through a simplified predictive model, named NeuralIMC.
elements.sent-2411-047: We start by introducing the basics of Internal Model Control (IMC) and our extension to integrate it with RL-based neural control (\cref{sec:imc ).}
elements.sent-2411-048: By leveraging the model-free RL algorithm PPO~\cite{schulman2017proximal , we reduce the complexity and accuracy requirements typically associated with classical model-based control.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Next, we detail how rigid transformation is used to predict the robot's body state, establishing a general approach to estimate disturbances and incorporate them into the control policy (\cref{sec:npm ).}
elements.sent-2411-050: Finally, we explain the application of this framework to two specific robot morphologies: quadrotors and quadrupedal robots (\cref{sec:implementation ).}

elements.sent-2411-051: Internal Model Control (IMC) is a powerful structure for robust control, offering significant advantages over classical feedback control, particularly in managing model uncertainties and external disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-052: Unlike classical feedback control, which relies on real-time error correction based on the difference between desired and actual outputs, IMC incorporates a predictive model of the process, allowing it to anticipate and compensate for disturbances more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-053: The structures of classical feedback control and IMC are illustrated in \cref{fig:control_structure , where $P$ and $\tilde{P}$ represent the plant and internal models, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The reference signal, disturbance, and system output are denoted by $r$, $d$, and $y$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-055: In IMC, the predicted response $\tilde{y}$ is subtracted from the actual response $y$, and the difference, $\tilde{d = \tilde{y - y$, is used as the input signal to the IMC controller.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Generally, $\tilde{d}$ is non-zero due to modeling errors and unknown disturbances not accounted for in the model.
elements.sent-2411-057: Consequently, IMC can provide more robust performance by continuously adjusting the control input according to the discrepancy between the predicted and actual responses.
elements.sent-2411-058: While the design procedure for IMC is straightforward when the plant system is easy to model~\cite{rivera1986internal , it becomes significantly more complex and challenging for systems with nonlinear dynamics or when accurate models are difficult to obtain~\cite{henson1991internal,bin2020approximate .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This complexity arises because methods like linearization or approximation often fail to capture the true behavior of nonlinear systems, leading to performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-060: Such a dependency on precise modeling limits its practical applications in robotic systems, such as quadrotors with cable-suspended payloads and legged robots on complex terrains, where achieving a robust and accurate system model is inherently challenging.
elements.sent-2411-061: To address these challenges, our method combines the strengths of the IMC structure with large-scale RL training.
elements.sent-2411-062: A simplified predictive model in IMC provides a rough but stable estimate of the system's response.
elements.sent-2411-063: Instead of relying solely on precise system modeling, our approach uses large-scale randomized simulations to learn a robust control policy that compensates for inaccuracies and simplified-out complexities.
elements.sent-2411-064: Through extensive training, the RL algorithm optimizes the control policy conditioned on predictive errors, enhancing robustness and performance in real-world scenarios where exact modeling is impractical.
elements.sent-2411-065: The predictive error reveals signals about external disturbances and internal characteristics of nonlinear dynamics, enabling more informative control decisions and effective policy learning.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Design of a Simplified Predictive Model
elements.sent-2411-066: The predictive model is crucial in our framework, providing the baseline needed to detect and compensate for system discrepancies and disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-067: Designing an effective predictive model involves two main challenges: accurately estimating the system response and ensuring the model's predictions closely match actual system behavior.
elements.sent-2411-068: Accurate estimation is essential for the feedback loop to reflect the true robot state, while alignment between predictions and real responses is critical for reliable performance under varying conditions.
elements.sent-2411-069: To streamline state estimation across diverse robots, we model only the rigid body state, excluding actuator- or limb-specific states.
elements.sent-2411-070: For robots equipped with monocular or binocular cameras and built-in IMUs, numerous visual(-inertial) odometry methods provide robust state estimations~\cite{qin2018vins,von2018direct,t265,NEURIPS2023_7ac484b0 , which can be used as the actual system response within the IMC structure.}
elements.sent-2411-071: By treating the robot as a rigid body and using Euclidean transformation to estimate the next state, we significantly reduce the modeling complexity, making it more practical and efficient.
elements.sent-2411-072: Here, we first present a general approach for formulating the predictive model and calculating the error, followed by specific adaptations for different robot morphologies in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-073: We assume that the model inputs include velocity or acceleration commands, and define the current body state as \(\mathbf{x _k = [\mathbf{p _k, \mathbf{q _k, \mathbf{v _k, \boldsymbol{\omega _k] \in \mathbb{R ^{13}\), where \(\mathbf{p _k\) is the position, \(\mathbf{q _k\) is the quaternion (orientation), \(\mathbf{v _k\) is the velocity, and \(\boldsymbol{\omega _k\) is the angular velocity.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The time interval between two steps is denoted as \(\Delta t\).
elements.sent-2411-075: The next position and velocity of the body can be computed via Newton's law~\cite{newton1687principia : \begin{equation}\label{eq:update_pos} \tilde{p}_{k+1} = p_k + v_k \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} a_k \Delta t^2, \label{eq:update_vel} \tilde{v}_{k+1} = v_k + a_k \Delta t.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:update_pos} \tilde{p}_{k+1} = p_k + v_k \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} a_k \Delta t^2, \label{eq:update_vel} \tilde{v}_{k+1} = v_k + a_k \Delta t.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-076: For rotation, we adopt a discrete update rule while handling singularities.
elements.sent-2411-077: First, we compute the change in angle \(\Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}\) with the angular velocity \(\boldsymbol{\omega _k\) and angular acceleration \(\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega _k\): \begin{equation}\label{eq:update_dang} \Delta \theta = \omega_k \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} \dot{\omega}_k \Delta t^2.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Next, we follow \cite{grassia1998practical to apply the exponential map to project the rotation from \(\mathbb{R ^3\) to \(SO(3)\), obtaining the corresponding quaternion \(\mathbf{q _{\Delta \theta}\): \begin{equation}\label{eq:update_dquat} q_{\Delta \theta} =  \left[\cos\left(\frac{\|\Delta \theta\|}{2}\right), \frac{\sin\left(\frac{\|\Delta \theta\|}{2}\right)}{\|\Delta \theta\|} \cdot \Delta \theta\right], \|\Delta \theta\|\leq \sqrt[4]{\epsilon_{machine}} \left[\cos\left(\frac{\|\Delta \theta\|}{2}\right), \left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\|\Delta \theta\|^2}{48}\right) \cdot \Delta \theta\right], otherwise \end{equation} Finally, we can predict the next quaternion and angular velocity as follows, where \(\otimes\) denotes quaternion multiplication: \begin{equation}\label{eq:update_quat} \tilde{q}_{k+1} = q_k \otimes q_{\Delta \theta}, \label{eq:update_omega} \tilde{\omega}_{k+1} = \omega_k + \dot{\omega}_k \Delta t.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We have established an explicit method to predict the next body state, assuming the required motion can be represented by commands at the acceleration or velocity level.
elements.sent-2411-080: Then, given the predicted body state \(\tilde{\mathbf{x _{k+1 = \left[\tilde{\mathbf{p _{k+1 , \tilde{\mathbf{q _{k+1 , \tilde{\mathbf{v _{k+1 , \tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega _{k+1}\right]\) and the actual body state \(\mathbf{x _{k+1}\), we compute the predictive error \(\tilde{\mathbf{d _{k+1}\), which is then fed into the policy to provide effective feedback signals. \begin{equation}\tilde{d}_{k+1} = \left[\Delta \tilde{p}_{k+1}, 1 - \cos{\tilde{\Theta}}, \Delta \tilde{v}_{k+1}, \Delta \tilde{\omega}_{k+1}\right]\end{equation} Here, \(\Delta \tilde{\mathbf{p _{k+1}\), \(\Delta \tilde{\mathbf{v _{k+1}\), and \(\Delta \tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega _{k+1}\) represent the differences in position, velocity, and angular velocity, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We use the cosine distance to quantify the difference between orientations, where \(\tilde{\Theta}\) is the angle difference between the two quaternions.
elements.sent-2411-082: In this section, we further explain how to derive velocity or acceleration commands for various robots, including quadrotors and quadrupedal robots.
elements.sent-2411-083: We demonstrate how these commands align with existing control methods, ensuring seamless integration into the policy learning process.
elements.sent-2411-084: Traditional quadrotor controllers often use multi-layered cascaded structures~\cite{px4controller , offering flexible options for integrating neural controllers.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Recent research shows that using collective thrust and bodyrate (CTBR) as the action space for neural controllers provides significant advantages~\cite{kaufmann2022benchmark, huang2023datt,kaufmann2023champion .}
elements.sent-2411-086: CTBR-based quadrotor controllers exhibit strong resilience to dynamics mismatches, effective domain transfer, and high agility and performance.
elements.sent-2411-087: This action space design is inherently compatible with our framework.
elements.sent-2411-088: We can directly use the bodyrate command from the policy to update the rotation, while the linear acceleration is computed using the collective thrust to translate the robot.
elements.sent-2411-089: Consequently, the predictive error, calculated from the previous policy output and the latest successive body states, can be seamlessly integrated into the current policy input without further modifications to the learning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-090: Unlike quadrotors, legged robots, particularly quadrupedal and bipedal robots, have garnered significant attention for RL-based controllers due to their higher control dimensions and the complexities of modeling interactions with diverse terrains~\cite{li2023learning, doi:10.1126/scirobotics.aau5872, kumar2021rma, li2024reinforcement, li2022bridging .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Typically, a neural controller for legged robots generates target actuator positions to achieve the desired body velocity, including forward velocity (\(v_x\)), lateral velocity (\(v_y\)), and yaw rate (\(\omega_z\)).
elements.sent-2411-092: To integrate our predictive model into the control loop, we use the reference velocity command as the model input instead of the high-dimensional actuator motions.
elements.sent-2411-093: By differentiating two consecutive reference velocity commands, we obtain the linear acceleration in x and y axes, and the angular acceleration for the yaw angle.
elements.sent-2411-094: In this way, we can apply the rigid transformation by setting other acceleration terms to zero, ensuring seamless integration into the existing policy structure.
elements.sent-2411-095: This method is compatible with other algorithms by simply adding a plug-and-play feedback loop to augment policy inputs.
elements.sent-2411-096: To evaluate our framework's performance and versatility, we conduct experiments on quadrotors and quadrupedal robots, comparing against state-of-the-art methods in each domain.
elements.sent-2411-097: Then, we perform ablation studies on the feedback structure and noise tolerance of the simplified predictive model.
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, we present sim-to-real deployment results on a quadrotor with two rope-suspended payloads, showcasing its effectiveness in enhancing system robustness.

elements.sent-2411-175: In this paper, we propose a novel framework, Neural Internal Model Control (NeuralIMC), that combines model-based control with RL-based neural control.
elements.sent-2411-176: By focusing on body dynamics and adopting the white-box rigid-body transformation, we bypass high-dimensional nonlinearity and employ a simplified explicit predictive model to form a closed-loop feedback structure.
elements.sent-2411-177: Integrating model-free RL algorithms allows the neural control policy to react according to the predictive error feedback.
elements.sent-2411-178: Our method demonstrates superior performance on quadrotors and quadrupedal robots, surpassing state-of-the-art control methods in both domains.
elements.sent-2411-179: Real-world deployment on a quadrotor with heavy payloads highlights the robustness of our approach in handling disturbances and adapting to varying dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-180: This work underscores the potential of our combined framework for enhanced robustness and adaptability in robotic systems and suggests a direction for more interpretable learning-based control systems in future research.. Limitations.

elements.sent-2411-200: In this section, we provide a detailed explanation of how we set up the simulation tasks for training and evaluating various controllers for both quadrotors and quadrupedal robots.
elements.sent-2411-201: We employ a customized quadrotor simulator to conduct our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-202: For detailed formulations on quadrotor dynamics, we refer readers to \cite{faessler2016thrust .}
elements.sent-2411-203: To ensure a broad spectrum of domain randomization, we compute the inertia parameters based on the randomized size and mass of the quadrotor.
elements.sent-2411-204: This approach prevents the generation of unrealistic configurations by avoiding the independent randomization of these physical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-205: We denote the arm length of the quadrotor as \( L \).
elements.sent-2411-206: The width and height of the quadrotor are given by \(\alpha_w L\) and \(\alpha_h L\), respectively, where \(\alpha_w\) and \(\alpha_h\) represent the width-to-length and height-to-length ratios.
elements.sent-2411-207: Suppose the mass of the quadrotor is \( M \), then the inertia matrix is computed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:inertia} \mathbf{I} = \frac{M}{12} \begin{pmatrix} L^2 (\alpha_w^2 + \alpha_h^2) 0 0 0 L^2 (\alpha_w^2 + \alpha_h^2) 0 0 0 L^2 (2\alpha_w^2) \end{pmatrix}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-208: We randomize the parameters of the quadrotor dynamics for both training and evaluation, ensuring that the evaluation range is wider than the training range.
elements.sent-2411-209: For tasks with seen dynamics, we generate dynamics parameters within the training range.
elements.sent-2411-210: Conversely, for unseen dynamics, we use the evaluation range to generate the parameters to test the generalizablity of learned controllers.

elements.sent-2411-211: Here, we explain the tasks used in our experiments, which include one set-point task and four tracking tasks.
elements.sent-2411-212: Visualized samples for each trajectory are shown in \cref{fig:circle,fig:poly,fig:star,fig:zigzag .}
elements.sent-2411-213: For all tasks, the controller frequency is set to 50 Hz, and each episode lasts for 10 seconds, i.e., 500 steps.
elements.sent-2411-214: TrackCircle: This task requires the quadrotor to track a specific circular trajectory with a radius of 0.5 m and a period of 2 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-215: This trajectory is the simplest to track due to its smoothness and cyclicity.
elements.sent-2411-216: TrackPoly: This task requires the quadrotor to track a random chained polynomial trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-217: Each trajectory consists of a series of random 5-degree polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-218: The starting point is $(x=0, y=0)$, and connection points between polynomials are randomly selected within the episode.
elements.sent-2411-219: Each polynomial lasts for a random duration between 1.5 and 4.0 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-220: We ensure the first, second, and third order derivatives are continuous at each point, resulting in a smooth trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-221: TrackStar: This task requires the quadrotor to track a random five-pointed star trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-222: The center of the star is $(x=0, y=0)$, and the quadrotor starts from the center, flying to one point to track the star-shaped trajectory cyclically.
elements.sent-2411-223: The quadrotor is expected to fly at a certain velocity between points, making this task challenging due to its infeasibility.
elements.sent-2411-224: TrackZigzag: This task requires the quadrotor to track a random zigzag trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-225: These trajectories are generated by randomly selecting time intervals between 1.0 and 2.0 seconds, generating waypoints after each interval, and linearly connecting each waypoint.
elements.sent-2411-226: The waypoints vary from -2 m to 2 m in the x, y, and z directions.
elements.sent-2411-227: This type of trajectory is the most challenging to track due to the highest randomness.

elements.sent-2411-247: Internal Model Control (IMC) is a robust control framework that outperforms classical feedback control in handling model uncertainties and disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-248: Unlike feedback control, which corrects errors in real time, IMC uses a predictive model to anticipate and compensate for disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-249: The reference, disturbance, and output are denoted by $r$, $d$, and $y$, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-251: IMC is straightforward when the plant is easily modeled~\cite{rivera1986internal but becomes challenging for nonlinear systems where approximations fail to capture true dynamics~\cite{henson1991internal,bin2020approximate .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: This reliance on precise models limits its use in robotics, such as quadrotors with suspended payloads or legged robots on uneven terrain, where accurate system modeling is difficult.

elements.sent-2411-292: In this paper, we propose Neural Internal Model Control (NeuralIMC), a framework that bridges model-based control with RL-based neural control.
elements.sent-2411-293: By leveraging body dynamics and a white-box rigid-body transformation, we avoid high-dimensional nonlinearity, using a simplified predictive model in a closed-loop feedback structure.
elements.sent-2411-294: Model-free RL enables the policy to react to predictive errors.
